title: Particles は and が (topic vs. subject)
explanationMd: は (pronounced *wa*, not *ha*, when used as this particle) marks the **topic** — what the sentence is about. が marks the **subject** — specifically what does or is something, often introducing new information. 私は学生です (Watashi wa gakusei desu) sets up 'as for me' and comments on it; 誰が来ましたか (Dare ga kimashita ka, 'who came?') asks specifically which person did the action. Most beginner sentences reach for は by default, and that's fine — が becomes important once you're contrasting or answering a 'who/what' question.

examples.reading: わたしはにほんじんです。

examples.translation: I like cats. (が marks 猫 as the thing that's likeable)

commonErrors.note: Question words asking 'who/what did X' almost always take が, not は.

drills.prompt: 何＿ありますか。 (What is there?)

title: Particle を — marking the direct object
explanationMd: を (pronounced *o*) marks the direct object of a verb — the thing the action is done to. It always comes right after the noun and right before the verb: 水を飲みます (Mizu o nomimasu, 'I drink water'). を almost never appears anywhere else, so once you can spot it, you can immediately tell which noun is on the receiving end of the action.

examples.reading: にほんごをはなします。

commonErrors.wrong: 水は飲みます (when meaning simply 'I drink water')
commonErrors.note: は isn't wrong here, but it shifts the meaning to 'as for water, I drink it' (implying contrast with something else) — を is the neutral, default choice for a plain direct object.

drills.prompt: コーヒー＿飲みます。 (I drink coffee.)

title: Particles に and で — location and direction
explanationMd: に marks a destination or a fixed point in time/space — where something *goes to*, or where something simply *is*: 学校に行きます (Gakkou ni ikimasu, 'I go to school'). で marks where an action *happens* — the scene of the activity: 学校で勉強します (Gakkou de benkyou shimasu, 'I study at school'). The quick test: does the sentence describe existing/arriving somewhere (に), or doing something while there (で)?

commonErrors.note: 食べる is an action performed at a place, so it needs で, not に.

drills.prompt: 学校＿日本語を話します。 (I speak Japanese at school.)

title: です/ます — the polite form
explanationMd: です attaches to nouns and adjectives, ます attaches to verbs, and together they're the default polite register you use with strangers, coworkers, and anyone you're not close to — the safe choice while learning. 私は学生です (Watashi wa gakusei desu, 'I am a student'); 食べます (Tabemasu, 'I eat/will eat'). Verbs go from their dictionary form to their ます-form by changing the final vowel: 食べる → 食べます, 飲む → 飲みます, 話す → 話します.

examples.reading: まいにち、みずをのみます。

commonErrors.note: です attaches to nouns/adjectives; verbs get their own polite ending (ます), never です stacked on the dictionary form.

title: い-adjectives vs. な-adjectives
explanationMd: Japanese has two adjective families that conjugate differently. い-adjectives end in い (大きい ookii, 'big'; おいしい oishii, 'delicious') and can go straight in front of a noun or directly before です. な-adjectives (好き suki, 'likeable'; 元気 genki, 'energetic') need な inserted before a noun — 好きな食べ物 (sukina tabemono, 'a food I like') — but drop the な before です. Mixing these up is one of the most common beginner slips.

examples.translation: It's a big house. (い-adjective, no な needed)

examples.text: 好きな食べ物は何ですか。
examples.reading: すきなたべものはなんですか。
examples.romaji: Sukina tabemono wa nan desu ka.
examples.translation: What food do you like? (な-adjective + noun)

commonErrors.note: な-adjectives need な before the noun they describe — 好き alone can't modify a noun directly.
い-adjectives never take な — that's exclusively for the な-adjective family.

title: Negation — ません, くない, じゃないです
explanationMd: Each word type negates differently in polite speech. Verbs: swap ます for ません — 食べます → 食べません. い-adjectives: drop the final い and add くない(です) — おいしい → おいしくない(です). Nouns and な-adjectives: add じゃないです (casual) or ではありません (more formal) — 学生です → 学生じゃないです.

examples.text: これはおいしくないです。

examples.reading: がくせいじゃないです。

commonErrors.note: The final い of the adjective is replaced by くない, not kept in front of it.

title: Past tense — でした and ました
explanationMd: です becomes でした in the past; ます becomes ました. 学生です → 学生でした ('I was a student'). 食べます → 食べました ('I ate'). The pattern is completely regular once you know the polite present form — no separate conjugation table to memorize.

examples.reading: きのう、みせでたべました。
examples.translation: Yesterday, I ate at a shop.

commonErrors.note: い-adjectives have their own past form (かった) — です doesn't carry the past tense for them the way it does for nouns.

title: Questions with か
explanationMd: Adding か to the end of any polite sentence turns it into a question — no word order change, no extra verb like English 'do/does'. これは何ですか (Kore wa nan desu ka, 'What is this?'). In casual speech か is often dropped and rising intonation alone does the job (これ、何？), but か is the safe, unambiguous default while learning.

examples.reading: おなまえはなんですか。

commonErrors.wrong: 何ですか？ですか is question mark redundant?
commonErrors.right: 何ですか (no ？ needed, but harmless if added)
commonErrors.note: か already marks the question grammatically; a written ？ is optional stylistic reinforcement, not required.

drills.prompt: Turn into a question: 学生です

title: Particle の — possession and description
explanationMd: の links two nouns, with the first describing or owning the second — the mirror image of English 'X's Y' or 'Y of X'. 私の名前 (watashi no namae, 'my name'), 日本の食べ物 (Nihon no tabemono, 'Japanese food' / 'food of Japan'). Once you have の, you can chain simple nouns into a huge range of descriptions without needing separate adjectives.

examples.reading: これはともだちのいえです。
examples.romaji: Kore wa tomodachi no ie desu.

commonErrors.note: の is required between the two nouns — Japanese doesn't allow simply stacking nouns the way some possessive-less constructions might suggest.
